allies.rationale_text: Systems-thinking helps antifragility identify how shocks travel through the whole structure instead of over-reading isolated events.
allies.source_quote: Systems thinking provides the methodology to understand that the whole system is greater than the sum of its parts.

allies.rationale_text: Experimentation gives antifragility a practical route to benefit from variation through many bounded tests rather than one brittle bet.
allies.source_quote: adopt a portfolio of experiments approach. Test every single possibility and systematically compare outcomes.

allies.rationale_text: First-principles-thinking keeps antifragility from inheriting brittle assumptions by stripping the problem back to facts and desired outcomes.
allies.source_quote: By isolating the fundamental facts, practitioners are forced to innovate solutions rather than relying on brittle, inherited frameworks.

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation-bias makes antifragility brittle because it resists learning from disorder that contradicts the current model.
antagonists.source_quote: Confirmation bias causes individuals to seek out information that validates current beliefs, leading to a closed or resistant mindset.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Antifragility conflicts with anchoring when planners underestimate cascade failure probability by anchoring on low individual-component failure rates.
structured_tensions.source_quote: antifragility vs anchoring: antifragility conflicts with anchoring (anchoring) when planners underestimate cascade failure probability by anchoring on the low individual-component failure rate, building systems optimized for normal conditions rather than stress events.

curation_notes.summary: The raw source is strongest on systems, experiments, and anti-brittleness through first-principles redesign, plus one explicit anchoring tension.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor risk-sprawl beyond the source-backed stress, experimentation, and anti-bias surface.
open_questions: Availability-style conflict is source-real, but confirmation-bias and anchoring were the cleaner bounded conflicts into the current graph vocabulary.
